content: When you store a serde_json::Value directly into a PostgreSQL jsonb column using sqlx, the database treats it as opaque binary data until you cast and parse it, which is inefficient for complex queries. The real problem is that raw jsonb columns prevent you from leveraging Postgres' native index support on individual fields because the data arrives unstructured, forcing every read to scan the entire record or perform expensive string matching operations in application code. You must wrap your serde_json::Value in sqlx::types::Json<T> before mapping it to a struct field so that sqlx knows how to handle the deserialization and indexing at runtime; this wrapper ensures the driver correctly translates the postgres jsonb type into your Rust representation without manual casting logic. If you ignore this requirement and try to map raw jsonb columns directly, you risk panicking during query execution because sqlx expects a concrete type like String or Vec<u8>, not an untyped blob. The performance implications are stark: without proper typing via Json<T> wrappers, your application cannot benefit from Postgres' pg_trgm extensions or GIN indexes for partial matching on nested objects, leading to O(n) scan times instead of O(log n) lookups as database scale grows. When querying these columns, you must treat the result as a serde_json::Value instance and then deserialize it into your domain model using the same deserialization logic you would use for any other JSON payload; this means your Rust code effectively becomes a JSON parser rather than a direct data binder when dealing with deeply nested structures. Memory-wise, storing large serialized objects in jsonb columns causes unnecessary duplication if you aren't careful about reference counting and ownership transfer between layers, especially since Postgres stores the entire tree in memory as a single blob until parsed. The correct approach is to define your Rust structs with fields of type sqlx::types::Json<T> where T matches your actual data structure, allowing sqlx to handle the conversion from jsonb to your concrete types automatically while preserving type safety and enabling efficient index creation on specific paths within the JSON hierarchy.
